mapping.a_element: sudden change (quench) of interaction parameter in the Falicov-Kimball model
mapping.b_element: perturbative similarity renormalization group transformation of the Hamiltonian

mapping.a_element: non-equilibrium dynamical mean-field theory used to solve the time evolution
mapping.b_element: perturbative expansion and projection onto effective basis states to derive an effective Hamiltonian

mapping.a_element: relaxation to a non-thermal steady state described by generalized Gibbs ensembles
mapping.b_element: diagonalization of the small effective Hamiltonian matrix to obtain exact bound state energy

mapping.a_element: collapse and revival behavior with period h/interaction in the steady state
mapping.b_element: accuracy of order 10% in bound state energy from a truncated perturbative expansion

brief_justification: While both papers involve transformations of Hamiltonians and derive effective descriptions (generalized Gibbs ensembles vs. renormalized Hamiltonians), the core mechanisms differ: Paper A focuses on non-equilibrium dynamics and steady-state relaxation after a quench, governed by time evolution and mean-field theory, whereas Paper B employs a perturbative renormalization group approach to construct an effective Hamiltonian for bound states, with no shared dynamical or statistical process.
